desc: Controls identities and resource permissions across systems.
Verify who you are, decide what you can access, log what you did.
Requires two or more factors: something you know, have, or are.
One set of credentials grants access to multiple systems or applications.
Port-based authentication standard; clients must authenticate before network access is granted.
Authentication framework used by 802.1X; supports many methods (PEAP, EAP-TLS...).
Encapsulates EAP inside a TLS tunnel so credentials are protected in transit.

full: Extensible Authentication Protocol – Transport Layer Security
desc: Certificate-based mutual authentication; considered one of the most secure EAP methods.
Centralized AAA server used for network access control.

full: Terminal Access Controller Access Control System Plus
desc: AAA protocol that encrypts the entire session payload; commonly used in Cisco environments.
Queries and modifies directory services (users, groups, objects).
Ticket-based network authentication protocol; a KDC issues TGTs to authenticate clients.
The Kerberos server that authenticates clients and issues Ticket Granting Tickets (TGTs).
Initial Kerberos token used to request service-specific tickets without re-entering credentials.
Framework of policies, hardware, software, and procedures for managing digital certificates.
Trusted entity that signs and issues digital certificates.
Verifies an applicant's identity before forwarding a certificate request to the CA.
File containing a public key and identity info submitted to a CA to obtain a certificate.
CA-published list of digital certificates that have been revoked before their expiry date.
Real-time protocol for checking whether a specific certificate has been revoked.
Cryptographic protocol securing data in transit; successor to SSL. Used by HTTPS.
Deprecated predecessor to TLS; vulnerable to POODLE and BEAST attacks. Should not be used.
Widely used asymmetric encryption algorithm based on the difficulty of factoring large integers.
Fast, widely used symmetric block cipher supporting 128, 192, or 256-bit keys.
Asymmetric cryptography using elliptic curves; achieves strong security with smaller keys than RSA.
Key exchange that generates a fresh key pair for each session, enabling Perfect Forward Secrecy.
DHE using elliptic-curve math; faster and provides PFS.
Session keys are independent; compromise of a long-term key does not expose past session traffic.
Legacy 56-bit symmetric cipher; considered broken due to its short key length.
Applies DES three times for added security; deprecated for performance and vulnerability reasons.
Family of cryptographic hash functions (SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-3) used for integrity verification.
128-bit hash algorithm with known collision vulnerabilities; broken for security use.
Combines a hash function with a shared secret to verify both integrity and authenticity.
Intentionally slow key-stretching function that makes brute-force attacks on passwords expensive.
Random value used in encryption to ensure identical plaintexts produce different ciphertexts.
Hardware chip providing secure key storage, attestation, and measured boot functionality.
Dedicated physical device used for enterprise-grade cryptographic processing and key management.
Encrypts an entire storage volume so data is unreadable without authentication (e.g., BitLocker).
OS security feature that randomizes memory addresses to make exploitation harder.
Marks memory pages as non-executable to block shellcode from running in data regions.
Wi-Fi security protocol using AES/CCMP; the current standard for most wireless networks.
Latest Wi-Fi security standard; replaces PSK with SAE for stronger handshake protection.
AES-based encryption mode used in WPA2 for per-frame wireless encryption.
Legacy replacement for WEP used in WPA; deprecated and no longer considered secure.
WPA3 handshake replacing PSK; resistant to offline dictionary attacks.
Simplified wireless pairing feature; vulnerable to PIN brute-force (Reaver attack). Disable it.
Creates an encrypted tunnel between two endpoints over an untrusted network.
Suite of protocols that authenticates and encrypts IP packets at the network layer.
IPsec protocol providing integrity and authentication only; does not encrypt payload.
IPsec protocol providing encryption, integrity, and optional authentication.
Legacy firmware that initializes hardware at startup before passing control to the OS.
Modern BIOS replacement; supports Secure Boot, faster startup, and GPT disks.
Reliable, connection-oriented transport protocol using a three-way handshake (SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK).
Connectionless, low-latency transport with no delivery guarantees.
Unencrypted web traffic protocol; data is transmitted in plaintext.
HTTP encrypted over TLS; the standard for secure web browsing.
Resolves human-readable hostnames to IP addresses.
Encrypted protocol for remote login, file transfer (SFTP), and tunneling.
Legacy file transfer protocol; sends credentials in plaintext. Use SFTP instead.
Secure file transfer that runs over SSH (not related to FTPS).
Plaintext remote terminal protocol; insecure. Replaced by SSH.
Standard protocol for sending email between mail servers.
SMTP wrapped in TLS/SSL for encrypted email submission.
Automatically assigns IP addresses, gateways, and DNS servers to network clients.
Monitors and manages network devices; traps are asynchronous alerts sent by agents.
Synchronizes clocks across devices; critical for log correlation and Kerberos authentication.
Windows file and printer sharing protocol; also used by ransomware like WannaCry (EternalBlue).
Microsoft protocol that provides a graphical desktop session over the network.
Signaling protocol for establishing VoIP and video calls.
Negotiates Security Associations and cryptographic keys for IPsec tunnels.
Maps private IP addresses to one or more public IPs at a router boundary.
NAT variant that multiplexes many private hosts through a single public IP using port numbers.
The broadcast name of a wireless network visible to client devices.
The MAC address of a specific access point (AP) radio interface.
Passively monitors traffic/host activity and generates alerts; does NOT block.
Passive sensor placed on a network segment to analyze traffic for anomalies or signatures.
Agent installed on an individual system to monitor local logs, files, and activity.
Inline device that detects AND actively blocks malicious traffic in real time.
Inline IPS placed in the network path to block malicious traffic before it reaches targets.
IPS agent on an endpoint that can actively block malicious local activity.
Centralized platform for log aggregation, correlation, alerting, and compliance reporting.
Automates security workflows and incident response actions using playbooks.
Agent-based platform providing endpoint telemetry, behavioral detection, and containment capabilities.
Monitors and restricts the movement of sensitive data to prevent unauthorized exfiltration.
Detects unauthorized changes to critical files, directories, or configurations.
Security software that detects, quarantines, and removes malware using signatures and heuristics.
Observable artifact or evidence suggesting a system has been breached (e.g., malicious IP, hash).
Structured process: Preparation → Identification → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Lessons Learned.
Documented procedures, roles, and playbooks for responding to security incidents.
Team responsible for receiving, tracking, and responding to computer security incidents.
Security-focused team that coordinates detection, investigation, and response to incidents.
Non-profit that publishes the OWASP Top 10 list of critical web application vulnerabilities.
Injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users to steal data or session tokens.
Inserts malicious SQL into application input fields to manipulate or dump the backend database.
Tricks an authenticated user's browser into making unwanted requests to a trusted site.

full: National Institute of Standards and Technology
desc: US agency that publishes cybersecurity frameworks and guidelines (NIST CSF, SP 800-53, etc.).
Publishes the CIS Controls (prioritized security actions) and CIS Benchmarks (hardening guides).
EU regulation governing how organizations collect, store, and process personal data of EU residents.

full: Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard
desc: Security standard required for any organization that stores, processes, or transmits cardholder data.
Any data that can be used alone or combined to identify a specific individual.
Health-related data protected by HIPAA; includes diagnoses, treatments, and insurance information.
Strategy ensuring critical business functions continue during and after a disruptive event.
Documented procedures for restoring IT systems and data after a disaster or outage.
Maximum acceptable duration of downtime before service must be restored.
Maximum acceptable amount of data loss, measured as time since the last good backup.
Expected monetary loss from a single occurrence of a risk: Asset Value × Exposure Factor.
Estimated frequency (per year) that a specific threat event is expected to occur.
Expected yearly financial loss from a threat: SLE × ARO.
Cloud model where the provider manages the full application stack; customer manages data and users.
Cloud model providing a runtime/platform; customer manages applications and data.
Cloud model providing virtualized compute, storage, and networking; customer manages everything above.
Security policy enforcement point between users and cloud services; provides visibility and control.
Logically isolated network segment within a public cloud provider's infrastructure.
